explicit_text: I am originally from Córdoba, Veracruz, Mexico, where I grew up in a small town surrounded by mountains and beautiful rivers. Life there was simple and active, and walking everywhere was part of my daily routine. The natural landscapes made me feel free and connected to my roots before I moved to the United States.
implicit_text: I am originally from a small town in Veracruz, Mexico, surrounded by mountains and beautiful rivers. Life there was simple and active, and walking everywhere was part of my daily routine. The natural landscapes made me feel free and connected to my roots before I moved to the United States.

explicit_text: I lived in Mexico until I was 17 years old, in a small town near Córdoba, Veracruz. Growing up there meant being close to nature and living an active lifestyle, mostly on foot. Moving from Mexico to the United States was a big change for me, especially in terms of language and culture.
implicit_text: I lived in my home country until I was 17 years old, in a small town near Veracruz. Growing up there meant being close to nature and living an active lifestyle, mostly on foot. Moving from my home country to the United States was a big change for me, especially in terms of language and culture.

explicit_text: When I was 17, I moved to Minneapolis, Minnesota, which was very different from where I grew up. Minneapolis became my home base after traveling around the United States for two years. I bought a bicycle there and found biking to be my main way of getting around, especially enjoying the summer and even the challenging winters.

This city became my home base after traveling around the United States for two years. I bought a bicycle there and found biking to be my main way of getting around, especially enjoying the summer and even the challenging winters.

explicit_text: Minnesota is where I settled when I first came to the United States, specifically in Minneapolis. The state’s cold winters make biking challenging, but I still ride my bicycle through snow and icy roads. Living in Minnesota taught me resilience and helped me embrace a new lifestyle different from my hometown in Mexico.

explicit_text: I came to the United States at 17 and spent two years traveling across the country by train, exploring different cities and cultures. The diversity I experienced—from San Jose to Nashville to New York City—opened my eyes to the many ways people live and speak English. This journey helped me grow confident and understand that being unique is what makes this country special.

explicit_text: During my travels around the United States, I stopped in San Jose, California, where I noticed Spanish music playing on the radio. This was one of the first places where I realized how diverse the country is, with different regions having their own cultural flavors. San Jose represented a connection to my Hispanic roots within the broader American landscape.

explicit_text: While traveling by train, I passed through Nashville, Tennessee, where I heard country music on the radio. This was a striking contrast to the Spanish music I heard in San Jose and the hip-hop in New York City. Nashville showed me the regional differences in culture and language across the United States.

explicit_text: New York City was one of the stops on my two-year journey across the United States, where I listened to hip-hop music on the radio. The city’s energy and diversity stood out to me as part of the broader mosaic of American life. Experiencing New York helped me appreciate how varied and unique each place in the country can be.

explicit_text: Back in Minneapolis and the surrounding Twin Cities area, I found a vibrant biking community, especially during the short summer months. Even when winter arrives with snow and ice, I continue biking, which surprises many people. The Twin Cities have become a place where I feel free and connected, much like my hometown in Mexico but with new challenges and joys.
